coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical study on the intercalation properties of hydroxyl anion for the reversible conversion of graphene quantum dots into carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Doroodmand, Mohammad Mahdi
Deylaminezhad, Morteza
coredata.dc:description: The intercalating property of hydroxyl anion for reversible conversion of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into carbon dots (CDs) by electrochemical techniques such as cyclic voltammetry (CV) was studied in detail. For this purpose, the three-electrode system was a Pt disk as counter, Ag/AgCl as reference and GQDs-modified Pt disk as working electrodes. Based on the results, among various anions such as Cl−, Br−, PO4 3−, PO3 3−, NO2 −, OH−, and NO3 − as well as different cations like Na+, K+ and Li+, only OH− can selectively intercalate nanocarbons during reversible conversion of GQDs to CDs. According to the cyclic voltammograms at potential the same as −0.2V (vs, Ag/AgCl) GQDs were converted into CDs. However reverse conversion of CDs into GQDs was simply occurred by continuous scanning the potential from +0.1 to −0.8V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Effective role of OH− as selective intercalating agent was further approved using some surface analytical methods such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM), BET, BJH as well as some spectroscopic techniques such as Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR). This study was applicable from several aspects such as i) in the intelligent controlling the morphology as well as the active surface area of nanocarbons during fabrication of carbon-based sensors ii) selective separation of OH− through the electro-filtration process, iii) selective exchange between OH− and other anionic species in the separation analyses and iv) controlling the bandgap (Fermi levels) between the inter-layers of graphene-based nanostructures through the intercalation process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Carbon nanodots
Hydroxyl anion
Intercalation
Surface analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of accumulation potential on the sensitivity of the peak
Effect of concentration of OH− on the sensitivity of the system
Mechanism of the interaction between OH− and GQDs
Interaction between the OH− and some anionic species

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the designed electrochemical cell.
Intercalating effect of various cations and anionic species at fixed conditions including: concentration +0.8M; accumulation potential −0.5V; accumulation time, 240s; and a scan rate of 100mVs−1.
Effect of different accumulation potentials including a) −0.5V; (b), −0.8V; (c), −1.0V; and (d), −1.2V using OH− (0.8M) as interacting agent at scan rate of 100mVs−1.
Nyquist plots at different accumulation potentials of −0.2, −0.5, and −0.8V (vs. Ag/AgCl) using 0.8M OH−.
Effect of different concentrations of OH−, scan rate: 100mVs−1, accumulation potential: −0.8V and accumulation time: 240s.
Diagram of peak current vs. different concentrations of OH− during interaction with GQDs. Data are the average of 4 independent analyses.
Effect of accumulation time during analysis of a) 1.5M and b) 1.0M OH− at fixed condition including scan rate: 100mVs−1 and accumulation potential: −0.8V.
Effect of scan rate on the interaction process during analysis of OH− 1.5M.
Plots of peak current vs. a) square root of scan rate and b) scan rate in the range from 10 to 260mVs−1 during analysis of 1.5M OH−.

Interaction between the OH− and NO3 −. Note) From CVs “a” to “e” the concentration of NO3 − is increased, while concentration of OH− is decreased. CV “f” shows the increasing effect in the OH− concentration and decreasing effect in the NO3 − concentration.
FT-IR spectra related to A) fresh GQDs, B) OH−-intercalated QGDs, C) OH−/NO3 − intercalated QGDs, and D) OH−/NO3 −/OH−-intercalated QGDs.
XRD patterns (A) and Raman spectra (B) of GQDs and CDs.
SEM images of a) initial GQDs, b) CDs produced during interaction of OH−, and C) GQDs produced from CDs during the reversible intercalation process.
TEM images of GQDs (A) and CDs (B and C).
XPS spectra of GQDs and CDs.
UV–vis. spectra of GQDs and CDs at a pH of 7.0 and 4.5.
Fluorescence spectra of GQDs, CDs and their mixture at pH7.0.
Traces of turbidity for GQDs, CDs and their mixture at pH7.0, (inset) photographic images.
Parameters obtained from BET and BJH analyses.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical study on the intercalation properties of hydroxyl anion for the reversible conversion of graphene quantum dots into carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The intercalating property of hydroxyl anion for reversible conversion of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into carbon dots (CDs) by electrochemical techniques such as cyclic voltammetry (CV) was studied in detail. For this purpose, the three-electrode system was a Pt disk as counter, Ag/AgCl as reference and GQDs-modified Pt disk as working electrodes. Based on the results, among various anions such as Cl−, Br−, PO4 3−, PO3 3−, NO2 −, OH−, and NO3 − as well as different cations like Na+, K+ and Li+, only OH− can selectively intercalate nanocarbons during reversible conversion of GQDs to CDs. According to the cyclic voltammograms at potential the same as −0.2V (vs, Ag/AgCl) GQDs were converted into CDs. However reverse conversion of CDs into GQDs was simply occurred by continuous scanning the potential from +0.1 to −0.8V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Effective role of OH− as selective intercalating agent was further approved using some surface analytical methods such as scanning electron microscopy (SEM), BET, BJH as well as some spectroscopic techniques such as Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR). This study was applicable from several aspects such as i) in the intelligent controlling the morphology as well as the active surface area of nanocarbons during fabrication of carbon-based sensors ii) selective separation of OH− through the electro-filtration process, iii) selective exchange between OH− and other anionic species in the separation analyses and iv) controlling the bandgap (Fermi levels) between the inter-layers of graphene-based nanostructures through the intercalation process.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent investigations in the synthesis of nanomaterials have diversified nanotechnology and introduced new applications such as in the optoelectronic and biomedical fields [1–10]. Generally, inorganic nanomaterials with dimensions from 1–100nm can be classified into different categories based on their geometric shape such as spherical (e.g., metal and metal oxide nanoparticles) [11], fibril-shaped (e.g., carbon nanotubes and metal wires) [12], and platelet-like (e.g., natural smectite clays, graphite, and graphene sheets) [1–10,13]. The miniaturization of materials to nanometer size often leads to increase the surface area-to-volume ratios and surface energies, consequently producing unique physical properties. All these properties reveal the strong capacity of nanomaterials for their use in various parts of science. However some nanomaterials such as pristine graphene materials are unsuitable for intercalation by large species such as polymer chains [14,15].

This challenge is related to the intrinsic behavior of these nanostructures such as graphene as a bulk material that often have a pronounced tendency for agglomeration with a polymer matrix [14,15]. It is likely that oxidation followed by chemical functionalization would facilitate the dispersion and stabilize graphene to prevent agglomeration [15,16]. Functional groups attached to graphene can be small molecules [17–20] or polymer chains [21–23]. Chemical functionalization of graphene is a particularly attractive target because it is able to improve the solubility and processability along with enhancement in the interactions with some organic polymers [24,25]. Considerable work has been carried out on the amination, esterification [25,26], isocyanate modification [27] and polymer wrapping [21–23] as routes for the functionalization of graphene. The electrochemical modification of graphene using ionic liquids has also been reported [28]. But in spite of the variety of these methods, the mentioned problem is still existed. It seems that focusing on some new methods such as intercalation technique can solve this problem.

Intercalation is a special type of inclusion compound formed by the insertion of molecules or ions into vacancies between the inter-layers of a layered material [29]. All the intercalating reactions are accompanied by a partial or total transfer of the charge to the lattice and cause a change in the interlayer distance (basal spacing) [29]. The first intercalates were prepared more than 150years ago, and the initial host lattice was graphite [29]. Based on this report graphite has been considered as the only material which permits the intercalation of molecules without a lone electron pair or with a deficiency of electrons [30].

Intercalation of elements or molecules at graphene/substrate interfaces can decouple the epitaxial graphene over layers from the substrate surfaces and recover the intriguing electronic properties of freestanding graphene [31–40]. Two contrast intercalation mechanisms have been suggested to explain intercalation reactions at the graphene/metal surfaces [41]. The first one is some elements and molecules, which strongly interact with carbon and are supposed to produce transient atomic-scale defects at the graphene lattice. Consequently the intercalant atoms are able to penetrate through the carbon graphene lattice [41]. This exchange intercalation mechanism is feasible for elements such as Si, Ni, and Fe [41]. Nevertheless these elements and species cannot activate the C–C bonds during diffusion to the graphene through the extended defects such as domain boundaries and island edges [41]. Whereas the other mechanism is based on some molecules such as H2O and CO that can diffuse into the nano-space between graphene and substrate. Consequently chemical reactions may even happen in the two-dimensional (2-D) nano-space under graphene sheets [42,43]. However to the best of knowledge no report has been found in the literature for controlling the morphology of nanomaterials.

The electrochemical intercalation has been limited only to the alkali metals such as lithium and sodium into the 2-D graphite in molten chloride that has been employed to produce nano-sized carbon materials like tubes and particles [44–48]. Among these nanostructures, quantum dots are considered as specific nanomaterials, whose selective production is important. Basically a quantum dot is a nanometer-sized nanostructure (usually a semiconductor) in which excitons are confined in all three-dimensional (3-D) dimensions. Among these quantum dots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene fragments that are small enough to cause exciting confinement and a quantum size effect. Typically GQDs have diameters below 20nm [49].

In these nanostructures to control some physical properties such as the bandgap of GQDs, a promising approach is applied to convert the 2-D graphene sheets into zero-dimensional (0-D) GQDs named as carbon nanodots (CDs), which are considered as the unique properties associated with both graphene and GQDs [50]. Consequently the extraordinary properties of CDs and GQDs can be adopted in different fields of sciences such as solar cells, fuel cells, hydrogen storage and evolution, etc. [51]. It seems that focusing on the intercalating properties of various kinds of anions and cations can provide the capability for controlling the morphology of these nanostructures. For this purpose hereby in this study for the first time, it is focused on OH− as a new kind of intercalating agent during selective and reversible conversion of GQDs into CDs.

All the reagents were from their analytical grades. GQDs and CDs were synthesized according to the procedure reported in our previous research [52]. Materials such as KCl, NaCl, KNO3, LiNO3, NaBr, KBr, NaOH, and KOH were purchased from Merck Company. Stock solutions (3.0M) of various cations and anions such as Na+, K+, Li+, Cl−, OH−, Br−, NO3 −, and MnO4 − were prepared via individually dissolving the required amount of salts such as NaCl, KCl, LiNO3, KNO3, NaBr, KBr, NaOH, KOH, and KMnO4 in 25.0mL volumetric flask using deionized water as solvent.

A novel electrochemical cell was designed using glass cylindrical tubing with a diameter of 5.0cm (height)×2.5cm (radius). A conventional three-electrode system was used, consisting of a bare or nanocarbon-modified Pt disk electrode (2.0mm diameter) as working, platinum disk as counter and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrodes. The counter and reference electrodes were introduced to the cell through a teflon tubing. Whereas the working electrode was positioned from the bottom of the glass cell. The inter-electrode distance was controlled via positioning the working electrode inside a glass flange. The cell assembly is schematically shown in Fig. 1 . All electrochemical modes including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and choronoamperometry were performed with a Potentiostat–Galvanostat, μAutolab type Ш. All experiments were conducted at 25±2°C. Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using Shimadzo (FTIR-8300) spectrophotometer. The phase composition between the synthesized CDs was also characterized by several techniques such as X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XD-3A diffractometer), Cu K radiation, 0.15418nm, turbidometer (HF instruments model DRT-100 D), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, KYKY-EM3200), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss-EM10C-80KV, 20kV), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 200-D), Raman spectrometry (Model #: BWS456), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XD-3A diffractometer), Cu Kα radiation, 0.15418nm, turbidometer (HF instruments model DRT-100 D), diffractometry (Shimadzu XD-3A diffractometer), UV–vis. spectroscopy (UV-3600, Shimadzu Co.) and Brunauer–Emmett–Teller/Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BET, BJH analyses).

To study the intercalation of GQDs, 0.01g of GQD powder was weighted and directly inserted on the surface of the Pt disk as working electrode (Fig. 1). Therefore the GQDs were modified on the surface of Pt electrode based on their gravity. Then 10.0mL of the 1.5M of NaOH solution was added to the electrochemical cell. Afterward by applying potential of −0.8V for 240s, the CVs were analyzed. The GQD-modified Pt disk as working electrode was adopted for evaluation of the interaction process using diffusion-controlled voltammetry.

Intercalation process is considered as one of the most important techniques during controlling the size and morphology of inorganic matrix such as graphite during formation of nanostructures [44–48]. The importance of intercalation is so high that this process can be adopted during selection of various nano-scaled shapes ranging from 3-D to the 0-D by the effect of exfoliation as maximum influence of the interacting species among the inter-layer of any matrix. In addition, the range of the applications of intercalating species has been only limited to some actions such as K+ and Na+, but effective role of different kinds of interacting species is important during selective controlling the morphology of any matrix [53].

In this study, among various kinds of studied species such as Na+, K+, Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, Li+, and Br−, only OH− is considered as selective interacting species during reversible conversion of GQDs into CDs. Moreover, parameters having strong influence on the intercalation process include: kind and concentration of the intercalating species, effect of applied potential on the interaction process, and interaction between the interacting species with other cationic and anionic species. In this study to have QGD-modified working electrode with enough mechanical stability, the three-electrode system should be adopted to study the interaction process by the diffusion-controlled voltammetry.

For evaluation of the interaction process on GQDs during their conversion into CDs, the interacting role of various cations and anions such as 0.80M concentration of Na+, K+, Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, Li+, Br−, OH−, etc. were electrochemically studied under similar conditions such as a scan rate of 100mVs−1 as shown in Fig. 2 . As exhibited sensitive and well-defined peak currents were observed for OH− at potential of −0.20V vs. Ag/AgCl. However no peak current was detected during introduction of various cations and anions. This result clearly pointed to the significant interaction between OH− and GQD matrix. However quantum mechanical calculations were needed to exactly evaluate the basis of the interaction OH− and GQDs, but this selective interaction was probably attributed to the interaction through the n–π or n–π* interaction between OH− and C=C bonds of GQDs. In addition comparted to other anions such as NO2 − (with bond angle of approximately 120° and oxygen charge density of −0.5 [54]) or NO3 − (with trigonal planer and oxygen charge density of −2/3 [54]), linear structure of OH− (bond angle: 180° and oxygen charge density: −1) simply caused its intercalation into the inter-layers of the GQDs that probably majored to the scission of C=C bond during generation of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of accumulation potential on the sensitivity of the peak
ce:section.ce:para: For further research about the characteristics of this peak, the effect of the accumulation potential on the sensitivity (peak current) of the observed peak was evaluated in detail. In this study the accumulation potential has been defined as the potential at which OH− as intercalating reagent was diffused into the GQD's layers in a fixed time (accumulation time). For this purpose, stripping voltammetric technique was selected via applying constant direct-current potentials such as −0.50, −0.80, −1.0 and −1.2V (vs. Ag/AgCl) by the use of OH− with concentration of 0.80M at 100mVs−1 scan rate and 240s accumulation time. According to the voltammograms in Fig. 3 , maximum sensitivity was observed for the accumulation potential of −0.8V. This result was also in good agreement with that evaluated using EIS. Based on the Nyquist plots shown in Fig. 4 , significant reduction was observed in the double-layer resistance during applying ~−0.8V, compared to that measured for other accumulation potentials such as −0.5 and −0.2V. This phenomenon again revealed the correlation between the characteristics of the observed peak and the accumulation potential during the introduction of OH−.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration of OH− on the sensitivity of the system
ce:section.ce:para: Another factor having important effect on the sensitivity (peak current) during the interaction between OH− ion and GQDs was the concentration of OH−. To investigate this effect, the CVs of GQDs from −0.6 to 0.2V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and scan rate of 100mVs−1 were studied during applying a fixed accumulation time (~240s) at −0.8V potential throughout the introduction of OH− with variable concentrations from 0.01 to ~3.0M. The results are shown in Fig. 5 . As clearly shown, the intensity of the peak current was promoted during enhancement in the concentrations of OH−. The relationship between peak current (ip ) vs. different concentrations of OH− is also shown in Fig. 6 . As exhibited, the higher the concentrations of OH−, the more insensitive signal is obtained. This enhancing effect was observed up to ~1.5M OH−, in which the peak currents are overlapped and reproducible responses (RSD%=1.11%, n=4) were received. Therefore ~1.5M OH− was selected as optimum concentration during interaction between OH− and GQDs.

Besides the concentration of OH−, the accumulation time was also considered as important parameter throughout evaluation of the effective role of OH− during controlling and changing the morphology of GQDs. For this purpose, the influence of accumulation time was evaluated at accumulation potential of −0.8V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at the scan rate of 100mVs−1 as shown in Fig. 7 .a. Based on the results (Fig. 7.a), partially reproducible signals (RSD%=1.14, %, n=7) were obtained at an accumulation time between 4 and 28min. Therefore minimum accumulation time having reproducible and sensitive response (i.e. 240s) was selected as optimum accumulation time.

For further reliability about the optimized OH− concentration (1.5M), the results related to the 1.5M concentration of OH− (Fig. 7.a) were compared to that of 1.0M OH− (Fig. 7.b). As clearly evaluated (Fig. 7.a and b), reproducible signal was observed for 1.5M concentration of OH−, which pointed to the accuracy of the optimized process (accumulation potential=−0.8V, accumulation time=240s, and [OH−]=1.5M) during interacting between OH− and the GQD layers.

To investigate the effect of scan rate on the characteristics of the interaction between OH− and GQD, the CVs of 1.5M OH− on the GQDs-modified Pt as working electrode were studied at different scan rates between 10 and 260mVs−1 as shown in Fig. 8 . As expected, the sensitivity of the peak current was promoted during enhancing the scan rate. However positive shift was observed in the position of peaks from ~−0.38 to ~−0.20V by enhancing the scan rate from 10 to 260mVs−1. In another word, less negative potential was needed to have effective interaction between OH− and GQD layers. This phenomenon indirectly revealed the influence of scan rate on the perturbation of the GQD layers as well as mass transfer of OH− that again pointed to the intrinsic property of OH− for interacting with GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the interaction between OH− and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the mechanism of the mass transfer, the relationships between the peak currents vs. both the square root of scan rate (v1/2, Fig. 9 .a) and scan rate (v, Fig. 9.b) were investigated in detail. As is shown in Fig. 9.a, the peak current increased linearly with the square root of scan rate (v1/2, s−1/2) in the range of 20–260 v1/2, s−1/2 with a mathematical equation of y=0.0002x−0.0006 (R2 =0.9981). Smaller linearity also existed between peak currents vs. scan rate with the correlation equation of y=1E−05x+0.0003 (R2 =0.9803). As shown, the presence of more linear correlation between peak currents and the square root of scan rate clearly suggested that, the kinetics of the intercalation process was controlled by diffusion mechanism. This mechanism clearly showed the importance of mass transfer of OH− throughout the electrochemically interaction with GQDs.

As explained in detail in the previous section, OH− was considered as suitable interacting agent through controlling the morphology of each GQDs and CDs through the mass transfer of OH− into the inter-layers of GQDs. But to have nanocarbon-based nanostructure with stable morphology, parameters possessing influence on the intercalation properties of OH− should be controlled. Therefore mass transfer of OH− was considered as a key role throughout controlling the morphology of GQDs and CDs. The mass transfer of OH− was approved during evaluation of the CVs as shown in Fig. 3. However this behavior was dependent to the accumulation time and potential as well as to the concentration of OH−. In this part for further evaluation of the mass transfer of OH−, analytical techniques such as continuous (repeated) CVs, EIS, BET/BJT analyses as well as image processing using SEM and TEM have also been adopted. Detail of these studies has been shown in the subsequent sections.

Continuous (repeated) CVs were also another method for evaluation of the interaction property of OH−. This behavior was clearly observed via following CVs at different time scales throughout using OH− with concentrations lower than about 1.0M. As clearly shown in Fig. 7.b, reduction process caused to have significant decrease in the peak current during applying negative potentials. Reduction of OH− at negative potentials was the main reason for this observation (Fig. 3). Based on the results narrowing the potential window between −0.6 and +0.1V, majorly limited the reduction in the peak current to the desorption process at low slow rates. Nevertheless this behavior was controlled after saturation of inter-layers of GQDs when using OH− with 1.5M concentration. This concentration probably prevented diffusion-based mass transfer of OH− from the GQD's inter-layer to the bulk solution (Fig. 7.a). This effect was considered as a suitable behavior for possessing nanocarbon with stable morphology. This event again pointed to the presence of a strong interaction between OH− and GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between the OH− and some anionic species
ce:section.ce:para: Another important factor for the fantastic behavior of OH− was its interaction with other anionic species such as NO2 −, NO3 −, Cl−, and MnO4 −. To study this interaction, initially OH− with 1.5M condemnation was intercalated with GQDs via applying −0.8V potential (vs. Ag/AgCl) at optimum accumulation time (240s). Then the interaction between OH− as interacting agent and other anionic species was studied by the evaluation of the peak current in the presence of at least 3-fold excess of other mentioned anionic species. According to the CVs shown in Fig. 10 , the intensity of the peak current was effectively decreased via interaction between OH− and any other anionic species.

Compared to the continuous CVs (Fig. 3), the decrease in the peak current was rapidly occurred during several scan rates that referred to the effective role of OH− by means of interaction with the tested anion. Based on the results (Figs. 3, 5, and 7) the peak height was strongly dependent to the amount of intercalated OH−. It seemed this interaction between OH− and other tested anionic species was probably based on the exchange process.

To further interpret this interaction, some analytical techniques were evaluated. However due to the various problems such as i) dependency of the exchange process to the electrical potential and ii) effective role of OH− with high concentration (1.5M), it was imposable to evaluate the exchange process by simple analytical methods such as titration, UV–vis. spectroscopy or turbidometry to directly evaluate the exchange process for other ions such as Ca2+, Cl−, etc. in the nanostructure layers. Therefore the electrochemical results were evidenced by spectroscopic techniques such as FT-IR, XPS, Raman, and XRD as well as by surface analyses using SEM and TEM. Detail of these analyses is as follows:Based on the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 11 .A), an absorption peak at frequency of 1651.0cm−1 was related to the C=O functional groups [55]. Whereas the absorption bands situated at 1527.5 and 1388.7cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric COO− [55], respectively. Comparing to this spectrum, interaction between high concentrations of OH− (~3.0M) and GQDs led to have a significant and sensitive absorption peak at 1458.1cm−1 (Fig. 11.B) that revealed the effective role of asymmetry COO−. This phenomenon was attributed to the interaction process between OH− and GQDs. For further approving the exchange between OH− and an anion species, NO3 − was selected as a selected probe. Treatment between hydroxyl-affected GQDs with NO3 − (as selected anionic probe) majored a reverse behavior including decrease in the peak intensity at 1458.1cm−1 and observation of an absorption peak at 1388.7cm−1 as shoulder (Fig. 11.C). Also, reversible behavior of OH− as intercalating agent was further approved via re-contacting the sample with OH− and observation of the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 11.D), which was similar to that initially estimated for the intercalating role of the OH− (Fig. 11.B). It should be noted that, the absorption peak positioned between 650 and 900cm−1 was probably related to the out-of-plane wagging of C–H bond [55]. The weak transitions at ~460cm−1 may be associated with water liberations [56]. Therefore all the electrochemical results were in good agreement with those of the FT-IR. It also should be noted that, due to the instability of the Griess reagent at strong basic conditions, it was impossible to quantitatively estimate the extent of the exchange between OH− and NO3 −, experimentally.

The XRD patterns of GQDs and CDs were also shown in Fig. 12 .A. According to the XRD patterns, the crystalline size of each GQDs and CDs was evaluated from X-ray line broadening using Debye–Scherrer formula as: D =0.9λ / ß 1/2 cos θ, where D was the average crystalline size, λ the X-ray wavelength used, ß 1/2 the angular line width at half maximum intensity and θ was the Bragg's angle [57]. In accordance with the peak of C(002) for λ =1.0Å, the average crystalline sizes of each GQDs and CDs were estimated to about 5 and 20nm, respectively.

The changes in the morphology of each GQDs and CDs were also characterized using Raman spectrometry as shown in Fig. 12.B. Based on the Raman spectra, nanocarbon-based material was clearly evaluated according to the intensity ratio (G/D ratio) of G-band (~1580cm−1) to D-band (~1350cm−1) [58]. Based on the height (intensity) of each G- and D-bands [58] in the Raman spectra (Fig. 12.B), the defect of CDs was evaluated to 1.57, which remarkably was higher than that obtained for GQDs (0.81). Based on the results CDs with plenty of edge planes had larger active surface area in comparison with GQDs, which possessed large amounts basal planes in their morphology. This analysis again pointed to the intercalating property of OH− for selective conversion between GQDs and CDs.

For better evaluation of this surface analysis, the structures of the samples were directly observed using SEM images as shown in Fig. 13 . Based on the SEM images, during intercalation between OH− and the GQDs the inter-layers of GQDs were changed into CDs with an average diameter of ~20nm. These results were also in good agreement with those obtained using TEM images as shown in Fig. 14 . Changes in the morphology of carbon nanostructures from GQDs (Fig. 14.A) into CDs with different magnifications (Fig. 14.B and C) were clearly observed using the TEM images. These results showed that, the estimated crystalline size of CDs was in good agreement with that obtained from the Raman spectra. Therefore change in the morphology of the nanocarbons was clearly evidenced during the intercalation process.

The XPS measurements were also adapted to further study the composition and surface state of the carbon nanostructures before and after the interaction process. Detail of these characterization has been reported in detail in our previous study [52] Briefly specific and high resolution scan of C1s (Fig. 15 .A) showed several peaks at ~290eV, respectively, which corresponded to C–C, C–O, C=O, and COOH, respectively [59]. Moreover, the O1s spectrum showed two peaks at ~530eV, which were attributed to C–O and C=O, respectively [60,61]. As seen in Fig. 15.B and C, the XPS exhibited a dominant graphitic C1s peak at ~284eV and O1s peak at ~532eV for nanocarbons [60,61]. According to the XPS spectra of C1s from the synthesized nanocarbons (Fig. 15), peak intensity observed at ~288eV revealed a noticeable increase, which indicated more oxidation groups (C–O, C=O) in the nanocarbons. Therefore based on the XPS characterizations OH− was again considered as intercalating agent for reversible conversion between GQDs and CDs.

To further characterization of the interaction between GQDs and CDs, other analytical techniques such as UV–vis. spectroscopy, fluorescence spectrometry, and turbidity were also adopted. These analyses were applied in some suspensions containing each GQDs and CDs. According to the UV–vis. spectra (Fig. 16 ), the synthesized nanostructures can be characterized via following the polyaromatic networks with limited size linked through π–π* during decoration with functional groups like hydroxyl, epoxy, and carboxylic groups, which were related to the structure of GQDs and CDs [62]. Based on the UV–vis. spectra of GQDs and CDs, the absorption band situated at 370nm was correlated to the π–π* transition of C=C bond. This effect pointed to the polyaromatic sp2-hybrid carbon network [62]. Consequently based on the UV–vis. spectra, graphite was considered as the self-assembly of definite polyaromatic network (graphene sheet) linked through π–π* interaction of C=C bond [63]. Whereas absorption peaks situated at 218 and 276nm wavelengths were related to the n→π* transition of C=O or C–OH bonds [62].

The spectroscopic results were also confirmed via interpreting the UV–vis. spectra at two different pH values such as 7.0 and 4.5 (Fig. 16). Based on to the spectra (Fig. 16), as pH increased from 4.5 to 7.0, the absorption of nanocarbons at 218nm was gradually disappeared, probably due to the deprotonation of C=O function groups. This effect also provided a small shift in the absorption band at 276nm wavelength. Whereas no significant change was observed in the absorption band at 370nm wavelength. As expected, only carboxyl acid (−COOH) and hydroxyl (−OH) groups could be influenced by the pH of medium. These results were considered as another proof for the morphologies of each QGDs and CDs.

The same sequence was also observed via comparison between the UV–vis. (Fig. 16) and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 17 ) at pH7.0. As was clearly shown in Fig. 17, the intensity of fluorescence at maximum emission wavelength at 220nm was promoted through the formation of fluorescent CDs, whereas passive behavior of GQDs majorly decreased the fluorescence intensity. The presence of significant difference between the fluorescence intensities was another proof for intercalation between GQDs and CDs.

Final evidence that was utilized to evaluate the intercalation process was the turbidometry. The turbidity of different samples containing each GQD, CD, and their mixture was observed in Fig. 18 . Based on the results major difference was again observed in the turbidity from GQDs into CDs. This difference was also evidenced by the image processing of the suspension as shown in the inset of Fig. 18. Therefore all these evidences pointed to this fact that, intercalating agent has effective role throughout controlling the morphology and active surface area of nanocarbons.

In this study, the intercalation property of hydroxyl anion for the reversible conversion of GQDs into CDs by the electrochemical technique such as cyclic CV has been studied. The intercalation process has also been considered as reversible process during controlling the morphology as well as surface area of nanocarbons such as GQDs and CDs. To the best of our knowledge, this study was the first research that reports selective role of OH− as appropriate intercalating agent via interaction with other anionic species. This study is applicable from several aspects such as i) in the intelligent management the morphology as well as the active surface area of nanocarbons during fabrication of carbon-based sensors ii) selective separation of OH− through the electro-filtration process, iii) selective exchange between OH− and other anionic species in the separation process, and iv) controlling the bandgap (Fermi levels) between the inter-layers of graphene-based nanostructures through the intercalation process.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the support of this work by the Shiraz University Research Council.
